turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the court this morning, Case 23-1127, Wisconsin Bell, Inc. v. United States Ex Rel. Heath.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: The False Claims Act, I will call it the FCA, protects federal programs and funds from fraud by imposing civil liability on anyone who, and I am quoting here, "knowingly presents or causes to be presented a false or fraudulent claim for payment." As one might expect, the government can itself pursue people who violate the Act by presenting false or fraudulent claims. But the FCA is unusual because it also enables private actors to bring suit against those wrong doers on the government's behalf, and then to share in any monetary recovery. Today's case asks us to decide whether a particular kind of request for money qualifies as a claim under the FCA so that such a suit can go forward. The request in question is for monies provided by the federal government's E-Rate Program which is short for Education Rate Program. The E-RateProgram gives subsidies to schools and libraries to pay for Internet and other telecommunication services. To finance the program, Congress required telecom carriers to regularly contribute to a fund called the Universal Service Fund. That fund is administered by a private not-for-profit corporation. It is responsible for collecting the carrier's contributions, and when a request is made, for distributing that money to schools and libraries in accordance with federal rules. The plaintiff in this case is Todd Heath who is a professional auditor, specializing in the telecommunications industry. He believes that a telecom carrier, Wisconsin Bell, defrauded the E-Rate Program out of millions of dollars. According to Heath, Wisconsin Bell charged schools more than it was allowed to under the program and that inevitably led the Universal Service Fund to make higher subsidy payments than it should have. To recover that money for the federal government and for himself, Heath brought this suit under the FCA. For the suit to succeed though, Heath has to show that the requests for E-Rate subsidies count as claims under the FCA's definition of that term. According to the part of the definition relevant here, a request for money counts as a claim only if the federal government, and I'm quoting "provides or has provided any portion of the money." So the question here is essentially whether the government provided any of the money used to pay E-Rate subsidies. Wisconsin Bell says, it did not. It says all the money in the E-Rate Program is private rather than federal. That's because Wisconsin Bell says the money comes from private telecom carriers' contributions and then is handled and dispersed by a private corporation. So even if Wisconsin Bell committed fraud, it's not the kind of fraud against the government that the FCA is concerned with. Today, we reject that argument. We hold that the requests for E-Rate subsidies in this case do indeed count as claims under the FCA, so that Heath suit alleging fraud can proceed. We arrive with at that conclusion because during the years Wisconsin Bell was allegedly committing fraud, the government itself transferred more than $100 million from the US Treasury into the Universal Service Fund, that is, into the pot of money used to pay E-Rate subsidies. About half that amount consisted of delinquent contributions that the Treasury Department obtained from carriers through debt collection efforts. The other half came from civil settlements and criminal restitution payments arising out of Justice Department efforts to police wrongdoing in the E-RateProgram. By collecting that $100 million, putting it into US Treasury accounts and then routing it to the fund, the government provided a portion of the money used to pay E-Rate program participants and that is all the FCA requires. The government doesn't have to have provided all of the money, justsome part of it, and because the government did so here by depositing $100 million from the US Treasury into the fund to pay for E-Rate subsidies, any request for those subsidies counts as a claim under the FCA. So Heath's FCA suit alleging fraud can go forward. The opinion of the court is unanimous. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion in which Justice Kavanaugh joins and Justice Alito joins in part. Justice Kavanaugh has filed a concurring opinion in which Justice Thomas joins.
